title: The case for keeping handwriting in primary schools
summary: Argue whether primary schools should keep teaching handwriting — and teaching it well — even as keyboards dominate adult life, or accept that handwriting belongs to the past.
topic: Whether primary schools should keep teaching handwriting
prompt: Write a 350–500-word argumentative essay about whether primary schools should keep teaching handwriting — and teaching it well, with daily practice and explicit letter formation — or whether the time would be better spent on keyboarding and other skills. Anchor in a real classroom setting or system. Use a 'what' cleft, a concession ('Granted,'), at least one passive construction, and one participle clause. Take a clear position. Avoid both nostalgia for cursive and techno-progress clichés.
hintStarters: Watching a class of eight-year-olds in a Bucharest primary school slowly form the letters of their first sentences with thick pencils, you notice that the pace at which the words appear on the page is…
What handwriting actually teaches a child, beyond the obvious skill of producing a legible page, is something a generation of curriculum reformers has been remarkably comfortable…
Granted, the world an eight-year-old will work in is one in which most writing will be done on a screen, and the case for spending hours on letter formation when…

hintVocab.meaningVi: cải cách chương trình giảng dạy

hintVocab.phrase: to drop a subject from the timetable
hintVocab.meaningVi: loại bỏ một môn khỏi thời khóa biểu
luyện tập lặp đi lặp lại
loại bỏ dần một kỹ năng
học có liên quan đến cơ thể
kết quả về khả năng đọc viết

hintVocab.phrase: to draft on paper before screen
hintVocab.meaningVi: phác thảo trên giấy trước khi chuyển sang màn hình

sampleText:
Watching a class of eight-year-olds in a Bucharest primary school slowly form the letters of their first sentences with thick pencils, you notice that the pace at which the words appear on the page is roughly the pace at which the children are actually thinking. The forming of an 'a' takes a second. The space between words is considered. A misformed letter is studied and rewritten. None of this is efficient. All of it is, in the most literal sense, learning to write.

What handwriting actually teaches a child, beyond the obvious skill of producing a legible page, is something a generation of curriculum reformers has been remarkably comfortable forgetting in the rush to put tablets on every desk. Letter formation forces the slow encoding of sounds into shapes — a one-to-one motor map between phoneme and gesture that typing cannot replicate. Children who learn to write by hand read more accurately, spell more accurately, and recall what they have written more reliably. The evidence is not new. It is simply unwelcome in conversations where the iPad has already been ordered.

Granted, the world an eight-year-old will work in is one in which most writing will be done on a screen, and the case for spending hours on letter formation when keyboarding will dominate adult life can sound like a defence of the candle against the lightbulb. Cursive in particular has lost most of its functional uses, and primary teachers struggling to fit basic numeracy into the day cannot be blamed for treating handwriting as the first item to compress when the timetable shrinks.

Even so, the trade is not what it appears. Time spent learning to form letters is not time taken from typing — it is time taken from a child's neurological investment in literacy itself, and replacing it with keyboarding does not produce equivalent learning. A child who composes by hand drafts more slowly, reads what they have just written more carefully, and revises with more friction than a child whose first writing surface is a screen. The screen produces faster output. The hand produces, on the same task, better thinking.

The defensible position is that primary schools should retain daily handwriting practice through at least the first four years of school, that legibility and pen grip should be taught explicitly rather than left to chance, and that keyboarding should be introduced later, on top of the manual base, rather than instead of it. A child who can write by hand can also type. A child whose first encounter with composition is a touchscreen can usually do neither well. What is being defended here is not nostalgia. It is the slow surface on which most of literacy actually gets built.

sampleAnnotations.phrase: None of this is efficient. All of it is, in the most literal sense, learning to write
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: Không điều nào trong đó hiệu quả. Tất cả nó, theo nghĩa đen nhất, là học viết

sampleAnnotations.phrase: Children who learn to write by hand read more accurately, spell more accurately, and recall what they have written more reliably

sampleAnnotations.phrase: The evidence is not new. It is simply unwelcome in conversations where the iPad has already been ordered

sampleAnnotations.phrase: the case for spending hours on letter formation when keyboarding will dominate adult life can sound like a defence of the candle against the lightbulb
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: lập luận cho việc dành hàng giờ vào việc tạo hình chữ cái khi đánh máy sẽ thống trị đời sống người lớn có thể nghe như sự bảo vệ ngọn nến chống lại bóng đèn

sampleAnnotations.phrase: Cursive in particular has lost most of its functional uses
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: Chữ viết tay nối nói riêng đã mất hầu hết các công dụng chức năng của nó

sampleAnnotations.phrase: primary teachers struggling to fit basic numeracy into the day cannot be blamed for treating handwriting as the first item to compress when the timetable shrinks
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: các giáo viên tiểu học đang vật lộn để đưa toán học cơ bản vào trong ngày không thể bị đổ lỗi vì coi viết tay là mục đầu tiên để nén lại khi thời khóa biểu thu hẹp

sampleAnnotations.phrase: the trade is not what it appears. Time spent learning to form letters is not time taken from typing — it is time taken from a child's neurological investment in literacy itself
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: sự đánh đổi không phải là như nó có vẻ. Thời gian dành cho việc học tạo hình chữ cái không phải là thời gian bị lấy từ việc đánh máy — đó là thời gian được lấy từ khoản đầu tư thần kinh của một đứa trẻ vào chính khả năng đọc viết

sampleAnnotations.phrase: replacing it with keyboarding does not produce equivalent learning
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: thay thế nó bằng đánh máy không tạo ra việc học tương đương

sampleAnnotations.phrase: A child who composes by hand drafts more slowly, reads what they have just written more carefully, and revises with more friction than a child whose first writing surface is a screen
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: Một đứa trẻ soạn thảo bằng tay phác thảo chậm hơn, đọc những gì chúng vừa viết cẩn thận hơn, và sửa lại với nhiều ma sát hơn so với một đứa trẻ mà bề mặt viết đầu tiên là một màn hình

sampleAnnotations.phrase: The screen produces faster output. The hand produces, on the same task, better thinking
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: Màn hình tạo ra đầu ra nhanh hơn. Bàn tay tạo ra, trên cùng một nhiệm vụ, tư duy tốt hơn

sampleAnnotations.phrase: primary schools should retain daily handwriting practice through at least the first four years of school, that legibility and pen grip should be taught explicitly rather than left to chance, and that keyboarding should be introduced later, on top of the manual base, rather than instead of it
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: các trường tiểu học nên giữ luyện tập viết tay hàng ngày trong ít nhất bốn năm đầu của trường, rằng tính dễ đọc và cách cầm bút nên được dạy rõ ràng thay vì để cho ngẫu nhiên, và rằng đánh máy nên được giới thiệu sau, trên nền tảng thủ công, thay vì thay thế nó

sampleAnnotations.phrase: A child who can write by hand can also type. A child whose first encounter with composition is a touchscreen can usually do neither well
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: Một đứa trẻ có thể viết tay cũng có thể đánh máy. Một đứa trẻ mà cuộc gặp gỡ đầu tiên với việc soạn thảo là một màn hình cảm ứng thường không thể làm tốt cả hai

sampleAnnotations.phrase: What is being defended here is not nostalgia. It is the slow surface on which most of literacy actually gets built
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: Điều đang được bảo vệ ở đây không phải là nỗi nhớ. Đó là bề mặt chậm mà trên đó hầu hết khả năng đọc viết thực sự được xây dựng

sampleGrammarNotes.title: 'What' cleft + 'remarkably comfortable forgetting' for soft indictment
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'What handwriting actually teaches a child, beyond the obvious skill of producing a legible page, is something a generation of curriculum reformers has been remarkably comfortable forgetting in the rush to put tablets on every desk' dùng cấu trúc 'What … is …' với cụm 'has been remarkably comfortable forgetting' để gán trách nhiệm một cách lịch sự cho các nhà cải cách. Trạng từ 'remarkably' và 'comfortable' tạo ra một sự buộc tội nhẹ — họ không quên một cách ngây thơ, họ quên một cách dễ chịu.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'What handwriting actually teaches a child, beyond the obvious skill of producing a legible page, is something a generation of curriculum reformers has been remarkably comfortable forgetting in the rush to put tablets on every desk' uses a what-cleft with the phrase 'has been remarkably comfortable forgetting' to assign responsibility politely. The adverb 'remarkably' and 'comfortable' produce a soft indictment — they did not forget naively, they forgot pleasantly.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Passive 'is studied and rewritten' / 'has already been ordered' for institutional momentum
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'A misformed letter is studied and rewritten' và 'It is simply unwelcome in conversations where the iPad has already been ordered' dùng passive với hai mục đích khác nhau: cái đầu tiên mô tả quá trình giáo dục cá nhân của đứa trẻ, cái thứ hai mô tả một quyết định mua sắm đã hoàn thành trước khi cuộc trò chuyện kết thúc. Sự đối lập tinh tế giữa hai cách dùng passive — học có ý thức so với mua hàng đã xong — là điển hình cho prose B2.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'A misformed letter is studied and rewritten' and 'It is simply unwelcome in conversations where the iPad has already been ordered' use the passive with two different purposes: the first describes the child's deliberate educational process, the second describes a procurement decision completed before the conversation has finished. The subtle contrast between these two passive uses — conscious learning vs concluded purchasing — is typical of B2 prose.

sampleTranslationVi:
Nhìn một lớp tám tuổi ở một trường tiểu học Bucharest từ từ tạo hình các chữ cái của những câu đầu tiên của họ với những chiếc bút chì dày, bạn nhận ra rằng tốc độ mà các từ xuất hiện trên trang giấy gần bằng tốc độ mà bọn trẻ thực sự đang suy nghĩ. Việc tạo hình chữ 'a' mất một giây. Khoảng cách giữa các từ được cân nhắc. Một chữ cái viết sai được nghiên cứu và viết lại. Không điều nào trong đó hiệu quả. Tất cả nó, theo nghĩa đen nhất, là học viết.

Điều mà việc viết tay thực sự dạy cho một đứa trẻ, vượt ra ngoài kỹ năng hiển nhiên là sản xuất một trang dễ đọc, là điều mà một thế hệ các nhà cải cách chương trình đã đáng kinh ngạc khi thoải mái quên trong sự vội vã đặt máy tính bảng trên mỗi bàn. Việc tạo hình chữ cái buộc việc mã hóa chậm của âm thanh thành hình dạng — một bản đồ vận động một-một giữa âm vị và cử chỉ mà việc đánh máy không thể sao chép. Trẻ em học viết bằng tay đọc chính xác hơn, đánh vần chính xác hơn, và nhớ lại những gì chúng đã viết đáng tin cậy hơn. Bằng chứng không mới. Nó chỉ đơn giản là không được hoan nghênh trong các cuộc trò chuyện nơi iPad đã được đặt mua.

Công nhận, thế giới mà một đứa tám tuổi sẽ làm việc trong đó là một thế giới mà hầu hết việc viết sẽ được làm trên màn hình, và lập luận cho việc dành hàng giờ vào việc tạo hình chữ cái khi đánh máy sẽ thống trị đời sống người lớn có thể nghe như sự bảo vệ ngọn nến chống lại bóng đèn. Chữ viết tay nối nói riêng đã mất hầu hết các công dụng chức năng của nó, và các giáo viên tiểu học đang vật lộn để đưa toán học cơ bản vào trong ngày không thể bị đổ lỗi vì coi viết tay là mục đầu tiên để nén lại khi thời khóa biểu thu hẹp.

Dẫu vậy, sự đánh đổi không phải là như nó có vẻ. Thời gian dành cho việc học tạo hình chữ cái không phải là thời gian bị lấy từ việc đánh máy — đó là thời gian được lấy từ khoản đầu tư thần kinh của một đứa trẻ vào chính khả năng đọc viết, và thay thế nó bằng đánh máy không tạo ra việc học tương đương. Một đứa trẻ soạn thảo bằng tay phác thảo chậm hơn, đọc những gì chúng vừa viết cẩn thận hơn, và sửa lại với nhiều ma sát hơn so với một đứa trẻ mà bề mặt viết đầu tiên là một màn hình. Màn hình tạo ra đầu ra nhanh hơn. Bàn tay tạo ra, trên cùng một nhiệm vụ, tư duy tốt hơn.

Lập trường có thể bảo vệ được là các trường tiểu học nên giữ luyện tập viết tay hàng ngày trong ít nhất bốn năm đầu của trường, rằng tính dễ đọc và cách cầm bút nên được dạy rõ ràng thay vì để cho ngẫu nhiên, và rằng đánh máy nên được giới thiệu sau, trên nền tảng thủ công, thay vì thay thế nó. Một đứa trẻ có thể viết tay cũng có thể đánh máy. Một đứa trẻ mà cuộc gặp gỡ đầu tiên với việc soạn thảo là một màn hình cảm ứng thường không thể làm tốt cả hai. Điều đang được bảo vệ ở đây không phải là nỗi nhớ. Đó là bề mặt chậm mà trên đó hầu hết khả năng đọc viết thực sự được xây dựng.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a 'what' cleft to expose what handwriting really teaches?
options: What handwriting actually teaches a child, beyond the obvious skill of producing a legible page, is something a generation of curriculum reformers has been remarkably comfortable forgetting in the rush to put tablets on every desk.
A generation of curriculum reformers remarkably comfortable forgetting what handwriting actually teaches a child is in the rush to put tablets on every desk.

Beyond the obvious skill of producing a legible page what handwriting actually teaches a child has been comfortable forgetting in the rush to put tablets on every desk is a generation of curriculum reformers.
Producing a legible page is the obvious skill what handwriting actually teaches a child something a generation of curriculum reformers in the rush to put tablets on every desk.
mcQuestions.explanation: The cleft 'What … is …' with the 'beyond the obvious skill' phrase rules out the surface answer before delivering the deeper one.

options: Learning conducted while seated in a chair
Learning in which the body — hand, gesture, muscle — participates directly, producing knowledge that is not equivalent to knowledge acquired through screen-only interaction
Learning to embody a character in a play
Learning carried out by a body of teachers
mcQuestions.explanation: Embodied learning treats the body as part of cognition; the essay argues that handwriting is one of the clearest cases of literacy as embodied learning.
mcQuestions.hint: Why 'embodied' as the opposite of 'disembodied' or 'screen-only'?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'Granted,' as a concession opener?
options: Granted, the world an eight-year-old will work in is one in which most writing will be done on a screen.
The world granted an eight-year-old will work in is one in which most writing will be done on a screen.
On a screen most writing granted will be done is one in which the world an eight-year-old will work in.
Will be done on a screen most writing the world granted an eight-year-old will work in is one in which.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Granted,' followed by a complete clause is the cleanest B2 concession opener.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option places 'Granted,' before a full sentence?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which passive construction places the misformed letter in subject position?
options: A misformed letter is studied and rewritten.
Studied and rewritten is a misformed letter.
A misformed letter studies and rewrites itself.
Studying a misformed letter is rewritten.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'is studied and rewritten' is a passive construction that puts the letter in subject position and backgrounds the child as the implicit agent.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option uses 'is studied' and 'is rewritten' as passives?

options: The memory of muscle injuries
Procedural skill stored in the body itself — gestures and movements that the hand can perform without conscious thought after practice
Memories about exercising
A type of brain disease affecting muscles
mcQuestions.explanation: Muscle memory is what handwriting builds — the hand learns to form letters without conscious deliberation, freeing attention for content.
mcQuestions.hint: What is 'memory' that lives in the 'muscle'?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence opens with a participle clause to set the analytical scene?
options: Watching a class of eight-year-olds in a Bucharest primary school slowly form the letters of their first sentences with thick pencils, you notice that the pace at which the words appear on the page is roughly the pace at which the children are actually thinking.

You notice watching a class of eight-year-olds in a Bucharest primary school slowly form the letters of their first sentences with thick pencils that the pace at which the words appear on the page is roughly the pace at which the children are actually thinking.

The pace at which the words appear on the page is roughly the pace at which the children are actually thinking watching a class of eight-year-olds in a Bucharest primary school you notice slowly form the letters of their first sentences with thick pencils.

Of their first sentences with thick pencils slowly form the letters watching a class of eight-year-olds in a Bucharest primary school you notice that the pace at which the words appear on the page is roughly the pace at which the children are actually thinking.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Watching a class of eight-year-olds in a Bucharest primary school…' is a present-participle clause opener that sets the analytical scene before the main clause delivers the observation.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for an -ing phrase at the start of the sentence, separated by a comma from the main clause.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which transition best signals a return to the main argument after a concession?
options: For example, all keyboards are bad.
Even so, the trade is not what it appears.
On the contrary, no child has ever written by hand.
Furthermore, all handwriting is perfect.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Even so' grants the previous concession (the future is screens) and pivots back to the writer's main argument by reframing the trade-off.
mcQuestions.hint: Which phrase both accepts the concession and reasserts the writer's broader position?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'memory encoding' mean in the essay's argument?
options: Encrypting computer memory
The cognitive process by which experience is converted into long-term memory — and which the essay argues is supported more reliably by handwriting than by typing
Memorising encoded messages
Storing memories on a USB stick
mcQuestions.explanation: Encoding is the storage stage of memory; the essay invokes evidence that handwriting produces more reliable encoding than typing.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence maintains the most formal academic register?
options: Honestly, handwriting is just basically how kids learn to read, no question.
It's kind of obvious that schools should keep pencils and stuff, full stop.

Daily handwriting practice in early primary education has been empirically associated with improved reading, spelling, and retention outcomes that keyboarding-only instruction does not consistently produce.
Writing by hand is like, you know, just better for kids and stuff.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option C uses formal vocabulary ('empirically associated', 'retention outcomes', 'keyboarding-only instruction') and avoids contractions, slang, and informal markers.

mcQuestions.prompt: What is the essay's central position?
options: Schools should ban all keyboards and tablets entirely.
Schools should drop handwriting and teach keyboarding only.

Primary schools should retain daily handwriting practice through at least the first four years, teach legibility and pen grip explicitly, and introduce keyboarding later on top of — not instead of — the manual base.
Schools should outsource handwriting practice to parents only.
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay defends a sequence: handwriting first, keyboarding added later — and rejects both the abolition of handwriting and the prohibition of screens.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at paragraph five for the writer's specific policy proposal.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option best paraphrases 'the slow surface on which most of literacy actually gets built'?
options: Paper that has been slowed down chemically
The handwriting page — slow precisely because it forces deliberation — that the essay argues is the foundation on which deeper literacy develops
A literacy that takes a long time to learn
A piece of furniture used for reading
mcQuestions.explanation: The 'slow surface' is the page worked on by hand — the friction is the feature, not the bug, of literacy formation.
mcQuestions.hint: What is 'the slow surface', and what does literacy gain from slowness?

options: A brand of pen with rubber finger pads
The specific way a child holds a pen — which, the essay argues, should be taught explicitly because incorrect grip becomes a lifelong handicap
The strength required to break a pen
Gripping someone's pen without asking
mcQuestions.explanation: Pen grip is one of the underrated mechanical skills the essay says schools should teach deliberately — otherwise it fossilises wrong and is hard to fix.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a defining relative clause correctly?
options: A child whose first encounter with composition is a touchscreen can usually do neither well.
First encounter with composition whose a touchscreen is a child can usually do neither well.
Do neither well usually a child can whose first encounter with composition is a touchscreen.
A touchscreen is a child usually can do neither well whose first encounter with composition.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'whose first encounter with composition is a touchscreen' is a defining relative clause specifying exactly which child the writer means.
mcQuestions.hint: Defining relative clauses follow the noun directly and start with 'who', 'whose', 'that', or 'which'.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'to phase out a skill' mean?
options: To remove a skill suddenly and completely
To gradually reduce or eliminate the teaching of a skill over time, often without an explicit decision to do so
To divide a skill into phases
To highlight a skill in the curriculum
mcQuestions.explanation: Phasing out is the slow erosion the essay warns against — handwriting is rarely formally cancelled; it just gets less time each year until it disappears.
mcQuestions.hint: What does 'phase out' do over time?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence closes the essay with a sharp, summary verdict?
options: In conclusion, handwriting is quite useful.
Therefore, all keyboards must be banned by law.
What is being defended here is not nostalgia. It is the slow surface on which most of literacy actually gets built.
To sum up, both pens and keyboards have advantages.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option C closes with a sharp 'not X. It is Y.' redefinition: the defence is not sentimental but structural.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option ends with a sharp 'not X. It is Y.' redefinition?

mcQuestions.prompt: According to the essay, what does a child who composes by hand do differently than a child who composes on a screen?
options: Writes faster and produces longer texts
Drafts more slowly, reads what they have just written more carefully, and revises with more friction — producing, on the same task, better thinking
Produces work that is harder to read
Asks for fewer breaks during writing time
mcQuestions.explanation: Paragraph four names exactly these three differences and frames them as cognitive benefits, not nostalgic ones.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at paragraph four for the writer's specific comparison.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Think of your own handwriting — when you last wrote a full page by hand, what changed about your thinking compared with typing the same content? If you were designing your child's first three years of primary school, how much daily handwriting would you protect, and what would you be willing to give up to make room for it?
